lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: These many, then, shall die; their names are prick’d.

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: Your brother too must die; consent you, Lepidus?

lines.speaker: LEPIDUS
lines.text: I do consent—

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: Prick him down, Antony.

lines.speaker: LEPIDUS
lines.text: Upon condition Publius shall not live,

lines.speaker: LEPIDUS
lines.text: Who is your sister’s son, Mark Antony.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: He shall not live; look, with a spot I damn him.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: But, Lepidus, go you to Caesar’s house;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Fetch the will hither, and we shall determine

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: How to cut off some charge in legacies.

lines.speaker: LEPIDUS
lines.text: What, shall I find you here?

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: Or here, or at the Capitol.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: This is a slight unmeritable man,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Meet to be sent on errands: is it fit,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: The three-fold world divided, he should stand

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: One of the three to share it?

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: So you thought him;

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: And took his voice who should be prick’d to die,

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: In our black sentence and proscription.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Octavius, I have seen more days than you:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And though we lay these honours on this man,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: To ease ourselves of divers slanderous loads,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: He shall but bear them as the ass bears gold,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: To groan and sweat under the business,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Either led or driven, as we point the way;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And having brought our treasure where we will,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Then take we down his load, and turn him off,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Like to the empty ass, to shake his ears,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And graze in commons.

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: You may do your will;

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: But he’s a tried and valiant soldier.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: So is my horse, Octavius; and for that

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: I do appoint him store of provender:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: It is a creature that I teach to fight,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: To wind, to stop, to run directly on,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: His corporal motion govern’d by my spirit.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And, in some taste, is Lepidus but so;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: He must be taught and train’d and bid go forth;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: A barren-spirited fellow; one that feeds

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: On abjects, orts and imitations,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Which, out of use and staled by other men,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Begin his fashion: do not talk of him,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: But as a property. And now, Octavius,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Listen great things:—Brutus and Cassius

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Are levying powers: we must straight make head:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Therefore let our alliance be combined,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Our best friends made, our means stretch’d;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And let us presently go sit in council,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: How covert matters may be best disclosed,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And open perils surest answered.

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: Let us do so: for we are at the stake,

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: And bay’d about with many enemies;

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: And some that smile have in their hearts, I fear,

lines.speaker: OCTAVIUS
lines.text: Millions of mischiefs.

stage_directions.text: Antony, Octavius, and Lepidus, seated at a table.

glosses.definition: marked off on the proscription list — each dot meant a death sentence and confiscation of property
the triumvirs bargain over kin — Lepidus consents to his brother's death in exchange for Antony's nephew; chilling political arithmetic

glosses.phrase: with a spot I damn him
glosses.definition: with a mark of the pen I condemn him to death

glosses.phrase: cut off some charge in legacies
glosses.definition: reduce the public bequests Caesar promised in his will — the same will Antony used in 3.2 to inflame the crowd
trivial, undeserving man

glosses.phrase: Meet to be sent on errands
glosses.definition: fit for running errands (nothing more)
the Roman world divided among the three triumvirs: Antony (east), Octavius (west), Lepidus (Africa). The Second Triumvirate, formed November 43 BCE
deadly sentence and proscription (= publicly-posted list of men to be killed with reward); Sulla invented proscription as a Roman political instrument; the triumvirs revived it in 43 BCE
various burdens of slander — the blame for proscription
the donkey carries treasure — a moral fable from Aesop; Lepidus is the beast of burden

glosses.phrase: empty ass, to shake his ears
glosses.definition: unburdened donkey shaking its ears — insulting image for a dismissed Lepidus
graze on common (public) pasture — demoted to nobody
plenty of fodder

glosses.phrase: wind, to stop, to run directly on
glosses.definition: turn, halt, gallop straight on — horseman's commands
bodily movement
rejected scraps, leftover morsels, and copied styles — what servants consume after their betters
made stale by others' prior use
a stage-property; a thing used and then discarded — Antony's cold theatrical metaphor
hear matters of importance
raising armies
raise a fighting force against them; take the field
hidden/secret matters
tied to the bear-baiting stake (the bear surrounded by dogs) — we are under attack
surrounded by baying hounds — the bear-baiting image continued

annotations.title: The Second Triumvirate and the proscriptions of 43 BCE
annotations.body:
The scene compresses one of Roman history's most brutal political moments. The Second Triumvirate — Antony, Octavius, Lepidus — was formally constituted by the *lex Titia* in November 43 BCE, granting them extraordinary power 'to restore the Republic' (the historical euphemism was itself already bitter). Their first act was the revival of Sullan proscription: posted lists of enemies to be killed, with rewards for killers and confiscation of property. Modern estimates give 2,000–3,000 wealthy Romans killed, including Cicero (December 7, 43 BCE) and much of the senatorial class.

Shakespeare telescopes the political horror into dinner-table bargaining: 'Your brother too must die; consent you, Lepidus?' The horror is the ease. The triumvirs trade deaths across the table — Lepidus's brother for Antony's nephew — the way they might trade provinces. Shakespeare's contribution to Roman political drama is this: he stages mass political killing as administration.

The scene also sets up the play's closing argument. The Republic the conspirators killed Caesar to preserve is being actively dismantled, twenty-odd lines after being saved. Within fifteen years, Octavius — sneered at by Cassius in 5.1 as 'a peevish schoolboy, worthless of such honour' — will be Augustus, first emperor of Rome. Shakespeare knows what his audience knows: the Republic is gone by the time the play ends, and the triumvirs' horse-trading in 4.1 is the mechanism of its end.

annotations.title: Antony on Lepidus: the horse-and-ass calculus
annotations.body:
Antony's assessment of Lepidus — 'a slight unmeritable man, / Meet to be sent on errands' — and the extended horse-and-ass metaphor mark the play's turn in Antony's characterisation. The grief-stricken friend of 3.1 and the masterly orator of 3.2 has become, in 4.1, an imperial calculator planning which partner to discard.

The metaphor is revealing. A horse is trained to fight, to wind, to stop, to run directly on. A donkey is bought for burdens and then turned out to graze. Antony is not offended by Lepidus being a donkey; he is methodical about it. The vocabulary of stage management ('a property') completes the shift: Lepidus is a prop, used and stored. Octavius offers a mild defence ('he's a tried and valiant soldier'), which Antony overrides. Shakespeare shows us the end of Antony's honourable phase. From here to 5.5, he is a political actor, not a moral one.

Historically, Antony's reading was correct in the short term. Lepidus was sidelined in 36 BCE when he was deprived of his army and retired to be pontifex maximus, a ceremonial office. He died in 13 or 12 BCE, still politically irrelevant. Antony's reading was also, however, wrong in the long term — it was not Antony who would discard Lepidus but Octavius, and the same Octavius would eventually discard Antony at Actium in 31 BCE. The horse-and-ass calculus Antony is applying to Lepidus is exactly the calculus Octavius is already applying to Antony.
sources: Paul Zanker, *The Power of Images in the Age of Augustus* (1988)

options: A fictional event invented for the play
The historical proscriptions of 43 BCE under the Second Triumvirate — 2,000–3,000 wealthy Romans killed, including Cicero, with reward for killers and confiscation of property

trials.prompt: Why is the scene staged with such cold, administrative tone ('with a spot I damn him')?
options: Because Shakespeare's argument is that mass political killing at this level is administration — horse-traded across a table — and the horror is the ease
Because the characters don't take it seriously

trials.prompt: Antony's extended horse-and-ass metaphor about Lepidus reveals:
options: A calculating, imperial Antony — the man who will now manage alliances like stable-animals, a full step away from the grief-stricken friend of 3.1; and ironically, Octavius is about to apply the same calculus to Antony himself

trials.prompt: What does 4.1 imply about the conspirators' political achievement?
options: They have failed in their goal — within twenty lines of the opening of Act 4 the triumvirs have revived proscription and are dismantling the senatorial class; the Republic the assassins killed Caesar to save is being actively killed in front of the audience
They have entered a peaceful interlude
